text: And last week we started a little mini series here on Wednesday evening as we get into the new year 2026. And someone reminded me today that we're almost halfway through the first month of the year. And I said, slow down, you know, slow down. And it's true. Tomorrow's the 15th. It's really amazing how quickly time flies. But last week we looked at just a single verse, Psalm 119, 126, it is time for the Lord to work for they have made void thy law. And I preached a message that I titled a longing to see God work. And as we examined that, that portion of scripture and made some points about it, we were encouraged to examine our own lives. And really, as we examine our own lives, we enter into this new year. The question is this, are we longing to see God work? Do we have that desire to see God do what only God can do? Do we recognize the ability that God has to work? The fact that he has all power, he is limitless, and he can do all things. He can just, he delights in answering prayer. And do we believe that God is able to work? And then we ask this question, do we recognize the need for God to work? We mentioned that the psalmist said they've made void thy law. And of course, we see, even in our own community and our own society, how they have disregarded the law of the Lord. And God's word is true, it's faithful, it's settled in heaven forever. And I am thankful that we have the Bible, but we understand that we need God to work. I ask this, we should have the desire that God would work in our lives individually. If you're married, that he'd work in your marriage. If you have a family, that he'd work in your home. That God would work in our church, in our community, in our commonwealth, in our nation, and in our world. And we really want to see God do what only God can do. And we took time to examine that. This evening, we're going to look at a portion of scripture in 2 Kings chapter number three. And as we examine this portion of scripture, we're going to note an event that took place in Israel's history. And really, we see Israel-Judah all together here. It's not a united kingdom, but we see both kings are involved in this particular passage of scripture. And by way of introduction, we're going to get through the whole chapter, Lord willing. But I want to draw your attention to verse number 18 as we get started. Because we've said that it's a longing to see God work and for God to do what only God can do, and that our God is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think. And I've simply titled the message, A Longing to See God Work, part two. We did part one last week, part two this evening, part three next week. But notice in verse number 18, this is Elisha the prophet speaking in verse number 18. And again, we'll catch it all in context as we go through the entirety of this chapter. But notice the Bible says this, and this is but a light thing in the sight of the Lord. He will deliver the Moabites also into your hand. And I want to focus on that phrase to begin verse number 18, where the Bible says, and this is but a light thing in the sight of the Lord. And we're going to look at that phrase and understand what happens here in this portion of Scripture and what God does. And we're longing to see God work and understand that God has all power. And when God does something miraculous, which he does in this text of Scripture, we find that Elisha says, this is but a light thing in the sight of the Lord. In other words, it's no problem for God. Your problem is no problem for God. Amen. Do you believe that tonight? Sometimes we look at things in our life, we say, but it's such a mountain. It's so big. And you don't understand. No, no, it's no problem for God. It's just a light thing. It's a small thing. It's a, it's no problem whatsoever. And we can rejoice in understanding that as we look at the portion of Scripture, and again, as we kind of walk our way through it. If you're taking notes, I want you to notice first of all, that they strategized. They strategized. In verses one through eight, we see how the strategy is being developed here. Thus I say they strategized and God gives us the setting in the first three verses. The Bible says in verse one, now Jehoram, the son of Ahab, began to reign over Israel and Samaria in the 18th year of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, and reigned 12 years. And he, Jehoram, wrought evil in the sight of the Lord, but not like his father and like his mother. His father and mother, that's Ahab and Jezebel, the wicked couple. And we find that it says, and not like, but not like his father and like his mother, for he put away the image of Baal that his father had made. However, we read in verse three, it says, nevertheless, he cleaved under the sins of Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, which made Israel to sin. He departed not therefrom. If you were to go back after the reign of Solomon, there's the divided kingdom, Jeroboam to the north, Rehoboam to the south, and Jeroboam instituted some wicked, just various wickedness in the northern kingdom with regard to offerings and holy days and all of that sort of thing. And we find that often throughout the pages of Scripture with regard to the history of Israel at this time, there will be reference back to the sins of Jeroboam. And we find that as God gives us the setting here in these first three verses of 2 Kings three, that we find that Jehoram is the king of Israel. He is the son of Ahab. We find that Jehoshaphat is the king of Judah. And Jehoshaphat has been reigning for 18 years, according to verse number one, when Jehoram becomes the king of Israel at the death of Ahab. We'll find as we go through the text of Scripture that Ahab died and there's some rebellion that takes place. But Elisha is the prophet of God during this particular time. The rebellion is recorded for us in verses four and five. The Bible says that Misha, king of Moab, was a sheep master and he rendered unto the king of Israel, that would be Ahab, and 100,000 lambs and 100,000 rams with the wool. Now that's a lot of product that was going into Israel. It was almost, if you were to tax of some sort, and we find that because of Ahab and because of his reign and the relationship there that the king of Moab, Misha, was sending this stuff back. And again, it says 100,000 lambs and 100,000 rams with the wool. But after Ahab dies and Jehoram becomes the king of Israel, it tells us in verse number five, but it came to pass when Ahab was dead that the king of Moab rebelled against the king of Israel. He decided, I'm not sending that stuff anymore. Now this would have a major impact, economical impact, on Israel, and of course, Jehoram's not happy about this. This would have been something that had been taking place and Jehoram is not happy about this rebellion. And here's where we really get into where they strategize because we find that what happens in verse number six is Jehoram begins to develop this strategy on what is he going to do? How is he going to respond because of this rebellion of Misha? The Bible says in verse six that King Jehoram went out of Samaria the same time, and it says this, and numbered all Israel. Now oftentimes, as you study again through the portion of history in Israel's history with regard to the kings and during that period of time, when the Bible will reference that the king went out and numbered his people, there was often a point for that, and that was that they were preparing for war. It was a situation in which they were understanding that I need to figure out how many people I have so that I can determine if I'm ready to go into battle. And so the Bible says that he, the king of Israel, Jehoram, that he went out of Samaria the same time and numbered all Israel. Not only did he number the people of Israel, but according to verse number seven, the Bible says that he went and sent to Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, and here's what he says. The king of Moab hath rebelled against me. Wilt thou go with me against Moab to battle? And he, Jehoshaphat, said to Jehoram, I will go up. As we study through the passage, we'll find that not only Jehoram, king of Israel, Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, but we find that the king of Edom is gonna join in with this coalition and it's gonna be these three kings that are gonna go up against Moab. And when Jehoshaphat asks the question in verse number eight, we find that Jehoram answers the question and says, the way through the wilderness of Edom. And so if you were to consider, and I don't have a map to show you in here, but if you were to consider the geography of Israel, you would find if you were to think about the Dead Sea being on the eastern part of the land of Israel, you would find that Moab would be just east of the Dead Sea and Edom would be south of that. And so they're gonna go south and then they're gonna make this, they're gonna go east and then back up north to attack Moab. And so we think about the way that they strategized and when I look at these first eight verses, I notice something really important that's missing. No one sought God. No one sought God. I'm gonna count my people and see how many I have. I'm gonna contact the king of Judah to come and to be with me and we're gonna take this direction, this is the route that we're gonna go in making our attack and they've strategized. They had to dig the ditches and then God did what they could not do and that's what we wanna see. We wanna see God do what we can't do. I don't wanna be satisfied. I don't wanna just have this perspective of what can we do. It's what can God do through us? Every once in a while, it's like the individual that comes to the Lord Jesus Christ and says, Lord, I believe, help thou mine unbelief. Or like the one that comes to Jesus during his earthly ministry and says this, Lord, if thou can't do anything, can I tell you, he can do anything. He can, that's the God we serve. And I wanna have and I want us as a church to have that longing to see God work, to get into the presence of God, to seek the face of God, to just inquire of the Lord that he would do a work that would without mistake, I mean, no one would be able to mistake the fact that it was God that did it because then God gets the glory and that's what it's all about. It's all about us as individuals and us corporately as a church, exalting and magnifying the name of the Lord Jesus Christ. It's like what John the Baptist said, he must increase, I must decrease. And when God works in the fashion in which we see in the scriptures, in this account that we've looked at this evening, God gets glorified, God gave victory. And so they got to see God work, I wanna see God work. I'm thankful we get to see God work. I've seen God do some great things, but I wanna see him do more. I don't wanna just be content in what he did yesterday, I wanna see him do great things in today and in our tomorrow and as long as he has us here that we continue to see God do great things. Remember, Elisha said, it's a light thing. It's a light, it was no problem for God. He said, man, that was a miraculous thing. Yeah, but for God, it was just a light thing. Our God is able, let's pray. Father, we love you. Thank you for loving us. Thank you for your word. Thank you for the truth. I thank you for the challenge. I pray God that you would help us to have the perspective of the psalmist that we looked at last week, a longing to see you work. God, I pray that you'd work in our lives individually, in our homes, our church, our community, our state, country, our world, or to the lives of our missionaries. God, that you would just continue to do great things. Thank you for the great things that you've done. I pray that you would continue to do great things. Thank you for the privilege of corporate prayer. I pray that you'd bless now throughout the remainder of the service, and we'll thank you for it. In Christ's name we pray, amen. Updates on the back of the bulletin from our missionaries.

segments.text: And last week we started a little mini series here on Wednesday evening as we get into the
new year 2026.
And someone reminded me today that we're almost halfway through the first month of the year.
And I said, slow down, you know, slow down.
And it's true.
Tomorrow's the 15th.
It's really amazing how quickly time flies.
But last week we looked at just a single verse, Psalm 119, 126, it is time for the Lord to
work for they have made void thy law.
And I preached a message that I titled a longing to see God work.
And as we examined that, that portion of scripture and made some points about it, we were encouraged
to examine our own lives.
And really, as we examine our own lives, we enter into this new year.
The question is this, are we longing to see God work?
Do we have that desire to see God do what only God can do?
Do we recognize the ability that God has to work?
The fact that he has all power, he is limitless, and he can do all things.
He can just, he delights in answering prayer.
And do we believe that God is able to work?
And then we ask this question, do we recognize the need for God to work?
We mentioned that the psalmist said they've made void thy law.
And of course, we see, even in our own community and our own society, how they have disregarded
the law of the Lord.
And God's word is true, it's faithful, it's settled in heaven forever.
And I am thankful that we have the Bible, but we understand that we need God to work.
I ask this, we should have the desire that God would work in our lives individually.
If you're married, that he'd work in your marriage.
If you have a family, that he'd work in your home.
That God would work in our church, in our community, in our commonwealth, in our nation,
and in our world.
And we really want to see God do what only God can do.
And we took time to examine that.
This evening, we're going to look at a portion of scripture in 2 Kings chapter number three.
And as we examine this portion of scripture, we're going to note an event that took place
in Israel's history.
And really, we see Israel-Judah all together here.
It's not a united kingdom, but we see both kings are involved in this particular passage
of scripture.
And by way of introduction, we're going to get through the whole chapter, Lord willing.
But I want to draw your attention to verse number 18 as we get started.
Because we've said that it's a longing to see God work and for God to do what only God
can do, and that our God is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think.
And I've simply titled the message, A Longing to See God Work, part two.
We did part one last week, part two this evening, part three next week.
But notice in verse number 18, this is Elisha the prophet speaking in verse number 18.
And again, we'll catch it all in context as we go through the entirety of this chapter.
But notice the Bible says this, and this is but a light thing in the sight of the Lord.
He will deliver the Moabites also into your hand.
And I want to focus on that phrase to begin verse number 18, where the Bible says, and
this is but a light thing in the sight of the Lord.
And we're going to look at that phrase and understand what happens here in this portion
of Scripture and what God does.
And we're longing to see God work and understand that God has all power.
And when God does something miraculous, which he does in this text of Scripture, we find
that Elisha says, this is but a light thing in the sight of the Lord.
In other words, it's no problem for God.
Your problem is no problem for God.
Amen.
Do you believe that tonight?
Sometimes we look at things in our life, we say, but it's such a mountain.
It's so big.
And you don't understand.
No, no, it's no problem for God.
It's just a light thing.
It's a small thing.
It's a, it's no problem whatsoever.
And we can rejoice in understanding that as we look at the portion of Scripture, and again,
as we kind of walk our way through it.
If you're taking notes, I want you to notice first of all, that they strategized.
They strategized.
In verses one through eight, we see how the strategy is being developed here.
Thus I say they strategized and God gives us the setting in the first three verses.
The Bible says in verse one, now Jehoram, the son of Ahab, began to reign over Israel
and Samaria in the 18th year of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, and reigned 12 years.
And he, Jehoram, wrought evil in the sight of the Lord, but not like his father and like
his mother.
His father and mother, that's Ahab and Jezebel, the wicked couple.
And we find that it says, and not like, but not like his father and like his mother, for
he put away the image of Baal that his father had made.
However, we read in verse three, it says, nevertheless, he cleaved under the sins of
Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, which made Israel to sin.
He departed not therefrom.
If you were to go back after the reign of Solomon, there's the divided kingdom, Jeroboam
to the north, Rehoboam to the south, and Jeroboam instituted some wicked, just various wickedness
in the northern kingdom with regard to offerings and holy days and all of that sort of thing.
And we find that often throughout the pages of Scripture with regard to the history of
Israel at this time, there will be reference back to the sins of Jeroboam.
And we find that as God gives us the setting here in these first three verses of 2 Kings
three, that we find that Jehoram is the king of Israel.
He is the son of Ahab.
We find that Jehoshaphat is the king of Judah.
And Jehoshaphat has been reigning for 18 years, according to verse number one, when Jehoram
becomes the king of Israel at the death of Ahab.
We'll find as we go through the text of Scripture that Ahab died and there's some rebellion
that takes place.
But Elisha is the prophet of God during this particular time.
The rebellion is recorded for us in verses four and five.
The Bible says that Misha, king of Moab, was a sheep master and he rendered unto the king
of Israel, that would be Ahab, and 100,000 lambs and 100,000 rams with the wool.
Now that's a lot of product that was going into Israel.
It was almost, if you were to tax of some sort, and we find that because of Ahab and
because of his reign and the relationship there that the king of Moab, Misha, was sending
this stuff back.
And again, it says 100,000 lambs and 100,000 rams with the wool.
But after Ahab dies and Jehoram becomes the king of Israel, it tells us in verse number
five, but it came to pass when Ahab was dead that the king of Moab rebelled against the
king of Israel.
He decided, I'm not sending that stuff anymore.
Now this would have a major impact, economical impact, on Israel, and of course, Jehoram's
not happy about this.
This would have been something that had been taking place and Jehoram is not happy about
this rebellion.
And here's where we really get into where they strategize because we find that what
happens in verse number six is Jehoram begins to develop this strategy on what is he going
to do?
How is he going to respond because of this rebellion of Misha?
The Bible says in verse six that King Jehoram went out of Samaria the same time, and it
says this, and numbered all Israel.
Now oftentimes, as you study again through the portion of history in Israel's history
with regard to the kings and during that period of time, when the Bible will reference that
the king went out and numbered his people, there was often a point for that, and that
was that they were preparing for war.
It was a situation in which they were understanding that I need to figure out how many people
I have so that I can determine if I'm ready to go into battle.
And so the Bible says that he, the king of Israel, Jehoram, that he went out of Samaria
the same time and numbered all Israel.
Not only did he number the people of Israel, but according to verse number seven, the Bible
says that he went and sent to Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, and here's what he says.
The king of Moab hath rebelled against me.
Wilt thou go with me against Moab to battle?
And he, Jehoshaphat, said to Jehoram, I will go up.
I am as thou art, my people as thy people, and my horses as thy horses.
Now understand with me just for a moment, we mentioned a little bit about Jehoram, Jehoshaphat
as the king of Judah.
If we were to say broadly speaking, Jehoshaphat's basically a good king.

on the part of Jehoshaphat.

and Jehoram is not happy about this rebellion of Mesha.

impact him.

that is being formed, and Jehoshaphat agrees.

So we find that Jehoshaphat agrees to, and they've made this strategy, but the strategy
continues to be developed, for we find that in verse number eight, there's a question
asked with regard to which way they would go up.
How would they enter into, how would they attack Moab?
So we find that in verse number eight.
he said, which way shall we go up?

they're gonna go east and then back up north
